rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute altered consciousness (confused, talking nonsense, barely rousable) in the context of massive continuous fluid loss and severe dehydration is an immediate threat to life — hypernatraemic encephalopathy with hypovolaemic shock. Telephone triage correctly escalated an acute change in mental status; the patient-reportable emergency feature (altered consciousness the carer can directly describe) justifies T5. The correct action is 000 ambulance for emergency hospital rehydration; deferring to routine hours risks seizures, coma and death.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Confusion, drowsiness or barely staying awake with ongoing heavy fluid loss
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 now — this cannot wait for clinic hours
escalation_edges.rationale: Altered consciousness signals hypernatraemic encephalopathy and imminent risk of seizures and coma; only emergency hospital care can safely rehydrate and correct the sodium.
escalation_edges.communication_template: He needs an ambulance right now — call 000 and tell them he is confused and drowsy after days of heavy urinating and can barely stay awake.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any temptation or advice to restrict his fluids
escalation_edges.rationale: Restricting fluids in this condition accelerates the crisis; the safe action is to keep offering sips and escalate, never to withhold water.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Do not stop his water — keep giving small sips while the ambulance is on the way.

escalation_edges.rationale: Seizure or unresponsiveness is deterioration of hypernatraemic encephalopathy needing immediate paramedic resuscitation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he has a fit, clear anything dangerous away, and once it stops roll him onto his side into the recovery position and tell the 000 operator straight away. If he stops breathing normally, they will guide you through what to do.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Patient is too drowsy to speak for himself; his spouse is the sole informant and carer at home out-of-hours
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Direct all instructions to the spouse in plain, calm steps — call 000, keep offering sips, do not restrict fluids, stay with him and unlock the door for paramedics.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Underlying inability to concentrate urine (suspected diabetes insipidus) means free-water loss continues regardless of intake
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Emphasise that fluids must never be withheld and that hospital assessment is non-negotiable even if he seems to rally briefly.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and say he is confused and barely awake after a week of extreme thirst and heavy urinating. Do not restrict his water — keep offering small sips while he can still swallow. Stay with him, keep him safe, and unlock the front door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary with the serum sodium and osmolality results and the diagnosis of the water-balance disorder; take this to the GP and any endocrine follow-up arranged.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as routine polyuria/thirst or dehydration that can wait for a GP or arranging an outpatient glucose test, rather than recognising altered consciousness as a 000 emergency.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Ongoing free-water loss drives serum sodium higher, causing seizures, coma, intracranial haemorrhage or death before he is seen.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising self-transport to the emergency department when altered consciousness and shock warrant an ambulance.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The exact serum sodium and the central-vs-nephrogenic cause cannot be determined remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate on the recognisable emergency (altered consciousness plus massive fluid loss) without waiting to confirm the biochemistry or the endocrine subtype.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Delaying escalation to gather more data, arrange outpatient tests, or determine the DI subtype over chat.
